questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: What physical quantity does the slope of a displacement-time graph represent?
questions.explanation: The slope of a displacement-time graph is \(\Delta x / \Delta t\), which is the definition of velocity. Option A (acceleration) is the slope of a velocity-time graph.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.content_anchor: slope of a position-time graph equals the velocity

explanations_tiered.definition: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement with respect to time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On a displacement-time graph, the slope (gradient) at any point gives the instantaneous velocity; a straight line gives constant velocity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you draw how far something goes over time, the steepness of the line tells you how fast it's moving.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Slope = \(\Delta x / \Delta t\). Since \(v = \Delta x / \Delta t\), the slope equals velocity.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous velocity \(v = dx/dt\) is the derivative of the displacement function, geometrically the slope of the tangent to the x-t curve.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of slope: vertical change divided by horizontal change.
Vertical axis is displacement (x), horizontal is time (t).
misconceptions: Some think slope gives acceleration; that's true only for v-t graphs.
A flat line means zero velocity, not zero acceleration necessarily.

glossary_entry.definition: Gives velocity; steeper slope = higher speed.

flashcard.front: What does slope of displacement-time graph represent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the slope of a displacement-time graph give?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Velocity (instantaneous or average depending on the interval).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: A displacement-time graph for a moving object is a parabola opening upward. What does this indicate about the motion?

options.B: The object moves with constant velocity
options.C: The object has constant positive acceleration
options.D: The object has constant negative acceleration

questions.hint: Recall \(s = \frac{1}{2}at^2\) for constant a from rest.
questions.explanation: For constant acceleration, displacement is proportional to \(t^2\), giving a parabolic s-t graph opening upward (positive curvature). Option B (constant velocity) gives a straight line.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.structural_element: b. Displacement-time graph for uniformly accelerated motion
source_trace.content_anchor: graph of displacement against time is a parabola (curved upward)

explanations_tiered.definition: A parabolic s-t graph (concave up) indicates constant positive acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When acceleration is constant, displacement is a quadratic function of time, producing a parabola.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the curve on a distance-time graph gets steeper, the object is speeding up (positive acceleration).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For uniformly accelerated motion, \(s = v_i t + \frac12 a t^2\). With \(v_i=0\), \(s \propto t^2\), a parabola. Upward opening means positive a.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Second derivative of s(t) is acceleration. For a parabola \(s = kt^2\), \(d^2s/dt^2 = 2k\), constant and positive if k>0.
reasoning_steps: Recall shape of s-t for constant a: parabola.
Upward opening means increasing slope → increasing velocity → positive a.
misconceptions: A parabola could also come from negative a if initial velocity is large enough? Actually concave up always means a>0 for a quadratic.
Straight line means constant velocity, not zero acceleration necessarily.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Upward parabola in s-t → positive constant a.

glossary_entry.definition: Indicates uniformly accelerated motion; displacement proportional to t².

flashcard.front: What motion does an upward-opening parabola on a displacement-time graph represent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Upward parabola in displacement-time graph means what acceleration?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Parabola opening upward → constant positive a.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Relate to s = ½ a t² shape.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. s = ½ a t² gives upward parabola for a>0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: What physical quantity is represented by the slope of a velocity-time graph?
questions.explanation: Slope of a v-t graph = \(\Delta v / \Delta t\), which is the definition of acceleration. Option C (displacement) is the area under the v-t graph, not the slope.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.content_anchor: slope of a velocity-time graph gives the acceleration

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity with time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On a v-t graph, the steepness (slope) tells how quickly velocity changes — that's acceleration.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the line on a speed-time graph is steep, the object is changing speed quickly (large acceleration).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Slope = \(\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}\). Since \(a = \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}\), slope equals acceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Instantaneous acceleration \(a = dv/dt\) is the derivative of v(t), geometrically the slope of the tangent to the v-t curve.
reasoning_steps: Rise is Δv, run is Δt.
misconceptions: Some think slope gives displacement; that's area under v-t.
Flat line means zero acceleration, not necessarily zero velocity.

flashcard.front: What does slope of a velocity-time graph represent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does the slope of a velocity-time graph represent?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: What does the area under a velocity-time graph represent?
questions.explanation: The area under a v-t graph equals the integral of velocity with respect to time, which is displacement. Option A (acceleration) is the slope, not the area.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.3 Graphical Representation of Uniformly accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.content_anchor: The area under the velocity-time graph gives the displacement

explanations_tiered.definition: Displacement is the integral of velocity over time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On a v-t graph, the area between the curve and the time axis equals the displacement (change in position).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you draw speed vs. time, the space under the line tells you how far you went.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For constant velocity, area = v×t = displacement. For changing velocity, area = ∫v dt = displacement.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement \(\Delta x = \int_{t_i}^{t_f} v(t) dt\), which is exactly the area under the v(t) curve.
reasoning_steps: Recall that displacement = average velocity × time.
Area under v-t = integral of v dt = displacement.
misconceptions: Area gives distance only if velocity never negative; otherwise gives displacement (with sign).
Slope gives acceleration, not area.

flashcard.front: What does area under a velocity-time graph represent?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does area under a velocity-time graph represent?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Displacement (or distance if velocity is always positive).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize sign matters for displacement vs distance.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Area under v-t = displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is the value of acceleration due to gravity near Earth's surface?
questions.hint: g is an acceleration, so units must be \(\mathrm{m/s^2}\).
questions.explanation: Acceleration due to gravity is \(g = 9.8 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\) downward. Option A has units of velocity, not acceleration.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: Near the surface of the Earth its magnitude is constant and is equal to 9.8 m/s²

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration due to gravity \(g\) is the acceleration of an object in free fall near Earth's surface.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: g is approximately \(9.8 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\) downward, independent of mass when air resistance is negligible.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you drop something, it speeds up by about 9.8 m/s every second it falls.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: g = 9.8 m/s² means velocity increases by 9.8 m/s each second during free fall downward.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The exact value varies slightly with latitude and altitude, but \(9.8 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\) is the standard approximation.
misconceptions: Some think g = 9.8 m/s (velocity) or 98 m/s².
g is not dependent on mass.

flashcard.front: What is the approximate value of g near Earth's surface?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is g near Earth's surface?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. g is acceleration, units m/s².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A ball is dropped from rest from a height of \(44.1 \ \mathrm{m}\). How long does it take to hit the ground? (Use \(g = 9.8 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\))
questions.explanation: From \(s = \frac12 g t^2\), \(t = \sqrt{2s/g} = \sqrt{2 \times 44.1 / 9.8} = \sqrt{88.2/9.8} = \sqrt{9} = 3.0 \ \mathrm{s}\). Option A would give \(\frac12 g (2)^2 = 19.6 \ \mathrm{m}\), too short.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Time to fall from rest under gravity: \(t = \sqrt{2h/g}\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For free fall from rest, displacement is proportional to t², so time is proportional to √h.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drop a ball, the time to hit the ground depends on the square root of the height.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(y = \frac12 g t^2\) with y = 44.1 m, g = 9.8 m/s² gives t = 3.0 s.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write s = ½ g t².
misconceptions: Using t = √(2g/h) instead of √(2h/g) gives wrong units.
Forgetting to take square root gives t² = 9 → t = 9? No, that's option D.

flashcard.front: Drop ball from 44.1 m. Time to hit ground? (g=9.8)

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Dropped from 44.1 m, find time. g=9.8 m/s².
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Common error: using h = gt²/2 incorrectly solved.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A ball thrown vertically upward returns to its starting point. How does the time of ascent compare to the time of descent? (Neglect air resistance.)
questions.hint: Symmetry of motion under constant downward acceleration.
questions.explanation: Without air resistance, the upward and downward motions are symmetric. The time to rise from start to highest point equals the time to fall back to the start. Option D is incorrect because the equality holds for any initial speed.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: time to go up equals time to come down for symmetric free fall

explanations_tiered.definition: For free fall without air resistance, ascent and descent times are equal due to symmetry.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The constant downward acceleration g affects upward and downward motion symmetrically if starting and ending heights are the same.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you throw a ball straight up and catch it at the same height, the time going up equals the time coming down.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(v_f = v_i - gt\), at top v_f=0 → t_up = v_i/g. For descent, \(v_f = gt_down\) and by symmetry v_f (down) = v_i → t_down = v_i/g.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The equation \(y = v_i t - \frac12 g t^2\) is quadratic; the two roots (t=0 and t=2v_i/g) are symmetric about t = v_i/g.
reasoning_steps: At top, v=0 so t_up = v_i/g.
On return, displacement=0 → 0 = v_i t_total - ½ g t_total² → t_total = 2v_i/g.
Thus t_down = t_total - t_up = v_i/g = t_up.
misconceptions: Some think descent takes longer because final speed is higher (but it's equal).
Air resistance breaks symmetry.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: t_up = t_down for symmetric free fall (no air resistance).

glossary_entry.definition: Time up equals time down when start and end heights are equal and air resistance is negligible.

flashcard.front: Time up vs time down for ball thrown upward and caught at same height?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: For a ball thrown up and caught at same height, compare ascent and descent times.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Due to constant g and symmetry, both times equal v_i/g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A student argues: 'Heavier objects fall faster because gravity pulls harder on them.' Why is this incorrect?

options.A: Heavier objects have more inertia, canceling the larger force
options.B: Gravity does not act on heavier objects
options.C: Air resistance affects heavy objects more
options.D: Mass does not affect acceleration due to gravity

questions.explanation: Weight is \(mg\), so acceleration \(a = F/m = mg/m = g\), independent of mass. Heavier objects have larger weight but also larger inertia, so acceleration is the same. Option A is essentially correct but D is the direct principle.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: all objects dropped under the influence of gravity near Earth's surface fall toward Earth with this acceleration regardless of mass

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration due to gravity is independent of mass because \(a = g\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: While weight increases with mass, inertia also increases, so the acceleration remains constant at g.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Even though a heavy ball is pulled harder, it's also harder to move, so both light and heavy fall at same rate (no air).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From Newton's second law, \(a = F/m = (mg)/m = g\). Mass cancels, so acceleration is the same for all masses.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In vacuum, the only force is weight \(mg\), leading to \(a = g\) regardless of m. Galileo's experiment disproved Aristotle's claim.
reasoning_steps: Newton's second law: a = F/m = mg/m = g.
Thus a does not depend on m.
misconceptions: Believing larger force always means larger acceleration (ignoring inertia).
Confusing weight with acceleration.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: a = F/m = mg/m = g, independent of mass.

glossary_entry.definition: All objects in free fall have same acceleration g because mass cancels in a = F/m.

flashcard.front: Why do heavy and light objects fall at same rate (no air)?
flashcard.flashcard_back: Acceleration = weight/mass = mg/m = g, independent of mass.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why don't heavier objects fall faster in vacuum?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Because a = F/m = mg/m = g, so mass cancels.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: All masses accelerate at g in vacuum.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: a = g for all masses (no air).
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer D. Mass cancels in a = F/m = mg/m = g.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: In an experiment to measure g using free fall, a student measures height h and time t. Which graph should be plotted to obtain g from the slope?
questions.hint: For free fall from rest, \(h = \frac12 g t^2\).
questions.explanation: From \(h = \frac12 g t^2\), a graph of h vs t² is linear with slope \(\frac12 g\), so \(g = 2 \times \text{slope}\). Option A (h vs t) is parabolic, not linear.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: Draw a graph of h against t² and use slope to find g

explanations_tiered.definition: For free fall from rest, height is proportional to time squared: \(h \propto t^2\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Plotting h vs t² gives a straight line; slope = g/2.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you drop a ball and measure height and time, graphing height against (time × time) gives a straight line whose steepness relates to g.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(h = \frac12 g t^2\), \(h\) is linear in \(t^2\). Slope = \(\frac12 g\), so \(g = 2 \times \text{slope}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Linearization of quadratic data: plotting \(y\) vs \(x^2\) converts \(y = kx^2\) to \(y = k x^2\), a line through origin with slope k.
misconceptions: Plotting h vs t gives a curve, hard to get slope accurately.
Plotting h² vs t gives no direct linear relation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: h vs t² gives slope = g/2.

glossary_entry.definition: Plot h vs t² to find g from slope.

flashcard.front: To find g from free fall data, what should you plot against t²?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What graph gives g from free fall data?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Plot h vs t² to linearize.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. h = ½ g t² → linear with t², slope = g/2.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: If an object is thrown vertically upward with speed \(v_0\), what is its speed when it returns to the same height? (Neglect air resistance.)
questions.hint: Energy conservation or symmetry of motion under constant g.
questions.explanation: Due to conservation of mechanical energy (or symmetry of free fall), the speed at the same height is the same as the initial speed but directed downward. Option A is the speed at the top.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: speed when it returns to the same height equals initial speed

explanations_tiered.definition: For free fall without air resistance, speed at same height is equal to initial speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The motion is symmetric: ascent and descent are mirror images in time, so speed at equal heights is same magnitude.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you throw a ball upward at 10 m/s, it will come back down to your hand at 10 m/s (but downward).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2g\Delta y\) with Δy=0 gives \(v_f^2 = v_i^2\), so \(|v_f| = |v_i|\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: By conservation of mechanical energy, \(\frac12 m v_i^2 + mgh = \frac12 m v_f^2 + mgh\) → \(v_f^2 = v_i^2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Choose initial and final points at same height.
Step 2: Apply \(v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2a \Delta y\) with Δy=0.
misconceptions: Some think speed is zero (at top) or half initial.
Confusing velocity (direction matters) with speed (magnitude).

glossary_entry.definition: Speed equals initial speed when returning to same height in free fall without air drag.

flashcard.front: Ball thrown up at 15 m/s returns to same height. Speed?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Speed when ball returns to same height (no air resistance)?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize velocity changes sign but speed same.
